subject: Mathematics
q: The angle of the top of a vertical tower standing on a horizontal plane is observed to be <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from a point A on the plane. Let <math><mi>B</mi></math> be the point <math><mn>30</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> vertically above the point A. If the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from <math><mi>B</mi></math> be <math><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> , then the distance (in m) of the foot of the tower from the point <math><mi>A</mi></math> is:
question: The angle of the top of a vertical tower standing on a horizontal plane is observed to be <math><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from a point A on the plane. Let <math><mi>B</mi></math> be the point <math><mn>30</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> vertically above the point A. If the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from <math><mi>B</mi></math> be <math><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> , then the distance (in m) of the foot of the tower from the point <math><mi>A</mi></math> is:
solution:
tan45o=1=x+30y⇒x+30=y ......(i)
tan30o=13=xy⇒x=y3 ......(ii)
from (i) and (ii) y=153+3

subject: Mathematics
q: If the angle of elevation of a cloud from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> which is <math><mn>25</mn><mi>m</mi></math> above a lake be <math><msup><mrow><mn>30</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> and the angle of depression of reflection of the could in the lake from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi>o</mi></msup></math>, then the height of the cloud (in meters) from the surface of the lake is :
question: If the angle of elevation of a cloud from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> which is <math><mn>25</mn><mi>m</mi></math> above a lake be <math><msup><mrow><mn>30</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> and the angle of depression of reflection of the could in the lake from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi>o</mi></msup></math>, then the height of the cloud (in meters) from the surface of the lake is :
solution:
tan⁡30o=x-25d

and tan⁡60o=x+25d

⇒tan⁡30otan⁡60o=x-25x+25⇒13=x-25x+25

⇒x+25=3x-75

⇒2x=100⇒x=50m

subject: Mathematics
q: A tower stands at the centre of a circular park. $A$ and $B$ are two points on the boundary of the park such that $A B(=a)$ subtends an angle of $60^{\circ}$ at the foot of the tower, and the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from $A$ or $B$ is $30^{\circ}$. The height of the tower is
question: A tower stands at the centre of a circular park. $A$ and $B$ are two points on the boundary of the park such that $A B(=a)$ subtends an angle of $60^{\circ}$ at the foot of the tower, and the angle of elevation of the top of the tower from $A$ or $B$ is $30^{\circ}$. The height of the tower is
solution: $\triangle \mathrm{O A B}$ is equilateral $\therefore \mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{OB}=\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{a}$ Now $\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{h}{a}$ $\therefore h=\frac{a}{\sqrt{3}} \text {. }$

subject: Mathematics
q: The angle of elevation of a jet plane from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> on the ground is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> After a flight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> seconds at the speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>432</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hour</mi></math>, the angle of elevation changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> If the jet plane is flying at a constant height, then its height is:
question: The angle of elevation of a jet plane from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> on the ground is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> After a flight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> seconds at the speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>432</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hour</mi></math>, the angle of elevation changes to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> If the jet plane is flying at a constant height, then its height is:
solution:
tan60°=hy

3=hy⇒h=3y …1

tan30°=hx+y

13=hx+y⇒3h=x+y …2

Speed 432 km h-1⇒432×2060×60⇒125 km

3h=125+y

3h-125=y

from 1

h=33h-125

h=3 h-1235

h=635 km

h=12003 m

subject: Mathematics
q: $A B$ is a vertical pole with $B$ at the ground level and $A$ at the top. $A$ man finds that the angle of elevation of the point $A$ from a certain point $C$ on the ground is $60^{\circ}$. He moves away from the pole along the line $B C$ to a point $D$ such that $C D=7 \mathrm{~m}$. From $D$ the angle of elevation of the point $A$ is $45^{\circ}$. Then the height of the pole is
question: $A B$ is a vertical pole with $B$ at the ground level and $A$ at the top. $A$ man finds that the angle of elevation of the point $A$ from a certain point $C$ on the ground is $60^{\circ}$. He moves away from the pole along the line $B C$ to a point $D$ such that $C D=7 \mathrm{~m}$. From $D$ the angle of elevation of the point $A$ is $45^{\circ}$. Then the height of the pole is
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{AB}=7+x \\ & \text { Also } \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{x} \tan 60^{\circ}=x \sqrt{3} \\ & \therefore x \sqrt{3}=7+x \\ & \mathrm{x}=\frac{7}{\sqrt{3}-1} \\ & \mathrm{AB}=\frac{7 \sqrt{3}}{2}(\sqrt{3}+1) \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: An aeroplane flying at a constant speed, parallel to the horizontal ground, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> above it is observed at an elevation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> from a point on the ground. If after five seconds, its elevation from the same point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>, then the speed (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>) of the aeroplane is
question: An aeroplane flying at a constant speed, parallel to the horizontal ground, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> above it is observed at an elevation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> from a point on the ground. If after five seconds, its elevation from the same point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>, then the speed (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>hr</mi></math>) of the aeroplane is
solution:
Let from point C the angle of elevation of plane at B is 60°.

and after 5 seconds it reach at B'

In ∆ABC, AC=3cot60°=1

In ΔCA'B', A'C=3 cot30°=3

Hence, distance AA'=A'C-AC=3-1=2

Now, speed equals total distance travelled divided by total time taken.

Thus, speed =2560×60=2×60×605=72005=1440 km/hr

subject: Mathematics
q: A man is walking towards a vertical pillar in a straight path, at a uniform speed. At a certain point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> on the path, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>.  After walking for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> minutes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> in the same direction, at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> . Then the time taken (in minutes) by him, from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to reach the pillar, is
question: A man is walking towards a vertical pillar in a straight path, at a uniform speed. At a certain point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> on the path, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>.  After walking for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> minutes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> in the same direction, at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> . Then the time taken (in minutes) by him, from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to reach the pillar, is
solution:
ΔQPA :hx+y=tan30°

⇒3h=x+y ......i

ΔQPB :hy=tan60°

⇒h= 3y   ......ii

From equation i & ii, we get

3y=x+y 

⇒y=x2

∵ Speed is uniform

Therefore, distance x walked by him in 10 min.

and distance x2 walked by him in 5 min.

subject: Mathematics
q: The angle of elevation of a cloud <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math> above a still take is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math>. If the angle of depression of the image of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> in the lake from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi></math> (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>) is equal to
question: The angle of elevation of a cloud <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math> above a still take is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math>. If the angle of depression of the image of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> in the lake from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi></math> (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math>) is equal to
solution:
tan30°=xy=13⇒y=3x and 

tan60°=x+400y

So, x+400=3y=3x

2x=400, x=200

sin30°=200PC⇒PC=400

subject: Mathematics
q: A particle moves towards east from a point $A$ to a point $B$ at the rate of $4 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ and then towards north from $B$ to $C$ at the rate of $5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. If $A B=12 \mathrm{~km}$ and $B C=5 \mathrm{~km}$, then its average speed for its journey from $A$ to $C$ and resultant average velocity direct from $A$ to $C$ are respectively
question: A particle moves towards east from a point $A$ to a point $B$ at the rate of $4 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ and then towards north from $B$ to $C$ at the rate of $5 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. If $A B=12 \mathrm{~km}$ and $B C=5 \mathrm{~km}$, then its average speed for its journey from $A$ to $C$ and resultant average velocity direct from $A$ to $C$ are respectively
solution: Time $T_1$ from $A$ to $B=\frac{12}{4}=3 \mathrm{hrs}$ $T_2$ from $B$ to $C=\frac{5}{5}=1 \mathrm{hrs}$. Total time $=4 \mathrm{hrs}$. Average speed $=\frac{17}{4} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. Resultant average velocity $=\frac{13}{4} \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: A vertical pole fixed to the horizontal ground is divided in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> by a mark on it with lower part shorter than the upper part. If the two parts subtend equal angles at a point on the ground <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away from the base of the pole, then the height of the pole (in meters) is :
question: A vertical pole fixed to the horizontal ground is divided in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> by a mark on it with lower part shorter than the upper part. If the two parts subtend equal angles at a point on the ground <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away from the base of the pole, then the height of the pole (in meters) is :
solution:
Given that

A vertical pole fixed to the horizontal ground is divided in the ratio 3: 7 by a mark on it with lower part shorter than the upper part. If the two parts subtend equal angles at a point on the ground 18 m away from the base of the pole.

tanθ=x6
tan2θ=10x18=5x9

We know that tan 2θ = 2 tan θ1 - tan2θ

5x9=x31-x236

⇒  5=31-x236

1-x236=35

x236=25

⇒  x2=725  ⇒  x=625

From the figure ; 

 Length of pole =10x=6025×55

=60105=1210m

subject: Mathematics
q: An aeroplane flying at a constant speed, parallel to the horizontal ground, $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~km}$ above it, is observed at an elevation of $60^{\circ}$ from a point on the ground. If, after five seconds, its elevation from the same point, is $30^{\circ}$, then the speed (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{hr}$ ) of the aeroplane is
question: An aeroplane flying at a constant speed, parallel to the horizontal ground, $\sqrt{3} \mathrm{~km}$ above it, is observed at an elevation of $60^{\circ}$ from a point on the ground. If, after five seconds, its elevation from the same point, is $30^{\circ}$, then the speed (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{hr}$ ) of the aeroplane is
solution:
For $\Delta \mathrm{OA}, \mathrm{A}, \mathrm{OA}_1=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\tan 60^{\circ}}=1 \mathrm{~km}$
For $\Delta \mathrm{OB}_1, \mathrm{~B}, \mathrm{OB}_1=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\tan 30^{\circ}}=3 \mathrm{~km}$. As, a distance of $3-1=2 \mathrm{~km}$ is covered in 5 seconds. Therefore the speed of the plane is $$ \frac{2 \times 3600}{5}=1440 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is a triangular park with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A T.V. tower stands at the mid-point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math>. If the angles of elevation of the top of the tower at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are respectively, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, then the height of the tower (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>) is:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is a triangular park with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A T.V. tower stands at the mid-point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math>. If the angles of elevation of the top of the tower at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are respectively, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, then the height of the tower (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>) is:
solution:
Let the height of the tower MN be h

In ∆QMN

tan30°=MNQM

∴QM=3 h=MR  …(1){∵M is the point QM=MR}

Now in ΔMNP 

∵∠MPN=∠NMP=45°

∴MN=PM(∵ In a triangle sides opposite to equal angles are

equal...2 )

∴MN=PM=h

In ∆PMQ

By using Equation 1

PM=(200)2-(3 h)2   {By using Pythagoras theorem}

∴ From 2

h=(200)2-(3h)2

⇒h=(40000)-3 h2

⇒h2=40000-3 h2 {By squaring both the sides}

⇒4 h2=40000

⇒h2=10000 m

⇒h=100 m

subject: Mathematics
q: From the top A of a vertical wall AB of height 30 m, the angles of depression of the top P and bottom Q of a vertical tower PQ are 15∘ and 60∘ respectively, B and Q are on the same horizontal level. If C is a point on AB such that CB=PQ, then the area (in m2) of the quadrilateral BCPQ is equal to
question: From the top A of a vertical wall AB of height 30 m, the angles of depression of the top P and bottom Q of a vertical tower PQ are 15∘ and 60∘ respectively, B and Q are on the same horizontal level. If C is a point on AB such that CB=PQ, then the area (in m2) of the quadrilateral BCPQ is equal to
solution:
Given that from the top A of a vertical wall AB of height 30 m, the angles of depression of the top P and bottom Q of a vertical tower PQ are 15∘ and 60∘ respectively,

B and Q are on the same horizontal level andC is a point on AB such that CB=PQ.

The required diagram will be

From the diagram,

⇒tan60°=ABBQ

⇒30x=3;

⇒x=103 m

Also, tan15°=ACCP

⇒30-hx=2-3

⇒30-h=1032-3

⇒30-h=203-30

⇒h=60-203

Now area of the quadrilateral BCPQ is BC×BQ

∴ area=hx

=(60-203)103

=20033-3

=6003-1 m2

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> due north of it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> units due west of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>13</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced></math>. If the distance of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> from the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> units, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to
question: The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> due north of it is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> units due west of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>3</mn><msqrt><mn>13</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced></math>. If the distance of the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> from the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> units, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

The angle of elevation of the top of a tower from a point A due north of it is α and from a point B at a distance of 9 units due west of A is cos-1313. If the distance of the point B from the tower is 15 units, then cotα is equal to

Now making the diagram of given value we have,

Given OB=15

cosβ=313

So, tanβ=23

Now using the value of tanβ in △OPB we get, 

⇒tanβ=h15

⇒23=h15, so h=10

Now in △OAB

OA2+AB2=225

⇒OA2+81=225

⇒OA=12

Now in triangle △OAP

tanα=1012

⇒cotα=1210=65

subject: Mathematics
q: A spherical gas balloon of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> meter subtends an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> at the eye of the observer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> while the angle of elevation of its center from the eye of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. Then the height (in meter) of the top most point of the balloon from the level of the observer's eye is :
question: A spherical gas balloon of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> meter subtends an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> at the eye of the observer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> while the angle of elevation of its center from the eye of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. Then the height (in meter) of the top most point of the balloon from the level of the observer's eye is :
solution:
Assume, O is the  centre of sphere

and P,Q are the point of contact of tangents from A

Let T be top most point of balloon & R be foot of perpendicular from O to ground.

From triangle OAP, sin30°=16OA

⇒OA=16cosec30°=32

From triangle ARO, sin75°=OROA

⇒OR=OAsin75°

⇒32(3+1)22=86+82

So the height (in meter) of the top most point of the balloon from the level of the observer's eye is OR+OT

=8(6+2+2)

question: Two vertical poles are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart and the height of one is three times that of the other. If from the middle point of the line joining their feet, an observer finds the angles of elevation of their tops to be complementary, then the height of the shorter pole (in meters) is:
solution:
tanθ=h75=753 h

⇒  h2=7523

h=253 m

q: Let a vertical tower <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>h</mi></math> stands on a horizontal ground. Let from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> on the ground a man can see upto height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> of the tower with an angle of elevation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>α</mi></math>. When from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, he moves a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> in the direction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>P</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math>, he can see the top <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of the tower with an angle of elevation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mi>h</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to
question: Let a vertical tower <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>h</mi></math> stands on a horizontal ground. Let from a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> on the ground a man can see upto height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> of the tower with an angle of elevation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>α</mi></math>. When from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math>, he moves a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> in the direction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>P</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math>, he can see the top <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of the tower with an angle of elevation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo>=</mo><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mi>h</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given a vertical tower AB of height 2h stands on a horizontal ground and from a point P on the ground a man can see upto height h of the tower with an angle of elevation 2α. When from P, he moves a distance d in the direction of AP→, he can see the top B of the tower with an angle of elevation α. If d=7h, then tanα is equal to

Now on plotting the value in diagram we have,

​​​​​​​

Now in △PAC

tan2α=hx .....1

And in △HAB we have

tanα=2hx+7h

⇒tanα=2hhcot2α+7h from equation 1

⇒tanα=21-tan2α2tanα+7

Now put tanα=t and on simplifying we get,

⇒t=21-t22t+7

⇒t1-t22t+7=2

⇒1-t22+7t=2

⇒t2-27t+3=0

⇒t=7-2

⇒ tanα=7-2

q: A man on the top of a vertical tower observes a car moving at a uniform speed towards the tower on a horizontal road. If it takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> for the angle of depression of the car to change from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, then the time taken (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>) by the car to reach the foot of the tower is
question: A man on the top of a vertical tower observes a car moving at a uniform speed towards the tower on a horizontal road. If it takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>18</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> for the angle of depression of the car to change from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, then the time taken (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>min</mi></math>) by the car to reach the foot of the tower is
solution:
Let length of tower be h.

⇒AC′=AB=h

And AC=ABcot30°=3h

⇒CC′=3-1h

Time taken by car form C C'=18 min

⇒Time take by car to reach the foot of the tower

=183-1 min

=93+1 min

subject: Mathematics
q: Two ships A and B are sailing straight away from a fixed point $\mathrm{O}$ along routes such that $\angle \mathrm{AOB}$ is always $120^{\circ}$. At a certain instance, $\mathrm{OA}=8 \mathrm{~km}, \mathrm{OB}=6 \mathrm{~km}$ and the ship $\mathrm{A}$ is sailing at the rate of $20 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ while the ship B sailing at the rate of $30 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. Then the distance between $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is changing at the rate (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{hr}$ ):
question: Two ships A and B are sailing straight away from a fixed point $\mathrm{O}$ along routes such that $\angle \mathrm{AOB}$ is always $120^{\circ}$. At a certain instance, $\mathrm{OA}=8 \mathrm{~km}, \mathrm{OB}=6 \mathrm{~km}$ and the ship $\mathrm{A}$ is sailing at the rate of $20 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ while the ship B sailing at the rate of $30 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. Then the distance between $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is changing at the rate (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{hr}$ ):
solution: Let $\mathrm{OA}=x \mathrm{~km}, \mathrm{OB}=y \mathrm{~km}, \mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{R}$ $(\mathrm{AB})^2=(\mathrm{OA})^2+(\mathrm{OB})^2-2(\mathrm{OA})(\mathrm{OB}) \cos$ $120^{\circ}$ $$ \mathrm{R}^2=x^2+y^2-2 x y\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)=x^2+y^2+x y $$ $\mathrm{R}$ at $x=6 \mathrm{~km}$, and $y=8 \mathrm{~km}$ $$ \mathrm{R}=\sqrt{6^2+8^2+6 \times 8}=2 \sqrt{37} $$ Differentiating equation (1) with respect to $t$ $$ \begin{gathered} 2 R \frac{d R}{d t}=2 \frac{d x}{d t}+2 y \frac{d y}{d t}+\left(x \frac{d y}{d t}+y \frac{d x}{d t}\right) \\ =\frac{1}{2 R}[2 \times 8 \times 20+2 \times 6 \times 30+(8 \times 30+6 \times 20)] \\ \frac{d R}{d t}=\frac{1}{2 \times 2 \sqrt{37}}[1040]=\frac{260}{\sqrt{37}} \end{gathered} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: The angle of elevation of the top of a vertical tower from a point $\mathrm{P}$ on the horizontal ground was observed to be $\alpha$. After moving a distance 2 metres from $\mathrm{P}$ towards the foot of the tower, the angle of elevation changes to $\beta$. Then the height (in metres) of the tower is:
question: The angle of elevation of the top of a vertical tower from a point $\mathrm{P}$ on the horizontal ground was observed to be $\alpha$. After moving a distance 2 metres from $\mathrm{P}$ towards the foot of the tower, the angle of elevation changes to $\beta$. Then the height (in metres) of the tower is:
solution:
$$ \text { Let } \mathrm{AB} \text { be the tower of height ' } \mathrm{h} \text { '. } $$
Given : In $\triangle \mathrm{ABP}$ $\tan \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{PB}}$ or $\frac{\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha}=\frac{h}{x+2}$ $\Rightarrow(x+2) \sin \alpha=\mathrm{h} \cos \alpha$ $\Rightarrow h=\frac{x \sin \alpha+2 \sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha}$ Now, In $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}, \tan \beta=\frac{\mathrm{AB}}{\mathrm{BC}}$ $$ \Rightarrow \frac{\sin \beta}{\cos \beta}=\frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow x=\frac{h \cos \beta}{\sin \beta} $$ Putting the value of $x$ in eq. (2) to eq. (1), we get $$ \begin{aligned} &h=\frac{\frac{h \cos \beta \sin \alpha}{\sin \beta}+\frac{2 \sin \alpha}{1}}{\cos \alpha} \\ &\Rightarrow h=\frac{h \cos \beta \cdot \sin \alpha+2 \sin \alpha \sin \beta}{\sin \beta \cdot \cos \alpha} \\ & \end{aligned} $$ $\Rightarrow h(\sin \beta \cdot \cos \alpha-\cos \beta \cdot \sin \alpha)=2 \sin \alpha \cdot \sin \beta$ $\Rightarrow h[\sin (\beta-\alpha)]=2 \sin \alpha \cdot \sin \beta$ $\Rightarrow h=\frac{2 \sin \alpha \cdot \sin \beta}{\sin (\beta-\alpha)}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The angle of elevation of the top of a vertical tower from a point A, due east of it is <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> . The angle of elevation of the top of the same tower from a point B, due south of A is <math><msup><mrow><mn>30</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> . If the distance between A and B is <math><mn>54</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>m</mi></math> , then the height of the tower (in meters), is:
question: The angle of elevation of the top of a vertical tower from a point A, due east of it is <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> . The angle of elevation of the top of the same tower from a point B, due south of A is <math><msup><mrow><mn>30</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> . If the distance between A and B is <math><mn>54</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>m</mi></math> , then the height of the tower (in meters), is:
solution:
Let height of the tower be 'H' 
 Let AP=x
BP=y
In ∆APQ⇒tan⁡45o=Hx ⇒  H=x
In ∆BPQ⇒tan⁡30o=Hy  ⇒  y= 3 H

Since,  ∆APB is a right-angled triangle.
⇒x2+5422=y2     (Pythagoras Theorem )
⇒H2+5422=3H2
⇒5422=2H2
⇒542= 2H
⇒54=H

A vertical lamp post <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi></math> is erected at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>O</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>∠</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>O</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>O</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is the midpoint of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
question: A horizontal park is in the shape of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn></math>. A vertical lamp post <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi></math> is erected at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>O</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>∠</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>O</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>O</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is the midpoint of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, a horizontal park is in the shape of a triangle OAB with AB=16. A vertical lamp post OP is erected at the point O such that ∠PAO=∠PBO=15° and ∠PCO=45°, where C is the midpoint of AB. Then OP2 is equal to

Now plotting the graph of given value we get,

Now in △PAO {given ∠PAO=15°}

So, OPOA=tan15°  ⇒OA=OPcot15°

And in △PCO {given ∠PCO=45° }

So, OPOC=tan45°⇒OP=OC

Now, OP=OA2-82

⇒OP2=OP2cot215°-64

⇒OP2=OP23+13-12-64

⇒OP23+13-12-1=64

⇒OP2433-12=64

⇒OP2=3232-3

subject: Mathematics
q: If two vertical poles $20 \mathrm{~m}$ and $80 \mathrm{~m}$ high stand apart on a horizontal plane, then the height (in $\mathrm{m}$ ) of the point of intersection of the lines joining the top of each pole to the foot of other is
question: If two vertical poles $20 \mathrm{~m}$ and $80 \mathrm{~m}$ high stand apart on a horizontal plane, then the height (in $\mathrm{m}$ ) of the point of intersection of the lines joining the top of each pole to the foot of other is
solution: We put one pole at origin. $B C=80 \mathrm{~m}, O A=20 \mathrm{~m}$ Line $O C$ and $A B$ intersect at $M$. To find: Length of $M N$. Eqn of $O C: y=\left(\frac{80-0}{a-0}\right) x$ $\Rightarrow y=\frac{80}{a} x$ Eqn of $A B: y=\left(\frac{20-0}{0-a}\right)(x-a)$ $\Rightarrow y=\frac{-20}{a}(x-a)$ At $M:(1)=(2)$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{80}{a} x=\frac{-20}{a}(x-a) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{80}{a} x=\frac{-20}{a} x+20 \Rightarrow x=\frac{a}{5} \\ & \therefore \quad y=\frac{80}{a} \times \frac{a}{5}=16 \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is a trapezium such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> are parallel and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>⊥</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>D</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>θ</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>q</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is equal to
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is a trapezium such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> are parallel and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>⊥</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>D</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>θ</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>q</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Let ∠BDC=α, then

tanα=BCCD=pq

AE=BC=p

tanα+θ=AEED

⇒ED=ptanα+θ

=p1-tanαtanθtanα+tanθ

=p1-pqtanθpq+tanθ

Now, AB=q-ED

=q-pq-ptanθpq+qtanθ

=pq+q2tanθ-pq+p2tanθp+qtanθ

=p2+q2tanθp+qtanθ

=p2+q2sinθpcosθ+qsinθ

subject: Mathematics
q: The angle of elevation of the top of a hill from a point on the horizontal plane passing through the foot of the hill is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. After walking a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn></math> meters towards the top, up a slope inclined at angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal plane the angle of elevation of the top of the hill becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. Then the height of the hill (in meters) is _____.
question: The angle of elevation of the top of a hill from a point on the horizontal plane passing through the foot of the hill is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. After walking a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn></math> meters towards the top, up a slope inclined at angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal plane the angle of elevation of the top of the hill becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. Then the height of the hill (in meters) is _____.
solution:
x=80cos30°=403

y=80sin30°=40

tan45°=1

⇒  x+z=h

⇒  403+z=h  

tan75°=h-yz

⇒  2+3=h-40z

⇒  z=h-402+3

⇒  h-403=h-402+3

(1+3)h=80(1+3)

h=80

solution:
Equation of line OQ y=80ax

Equation of line AB xa+y20=1

Solving for Px0,y0,ay0/80a+y020=1

⇒y0180+120=1⇒y0=16

q: The angle of elevation of the top <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of a tower from the feet of one person standing due south of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> and from the feet of another person standing due west of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>. If the height of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> meters, then the distance (in meters) between the two persons is equal to
question: The angle of elevation of the top <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> of a tower from the feet of one person standing due south of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> and from the feet of another person standing due west of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>. If the height of the tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> meters, then the distance (in meters) between the two persons is equal to
solution:
Given that the elevation to the top of the tower from the feet of one person due south is =45°

Similarly the angle of elevation to the top of tower from the feet of another person to the west of tower is =30°

The given information can be represented in the form of a diagram as below:

We need to find the distance AB.

Let the distance of A,B from the tower be x1,x2.
⇒tan 45°=5x1, tan 30°=5x2
⇒x1=5, x2=53.

⇒AB=x12+x22=52+532=10 m

Hence this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: Two poles standing on a horizontal ground are of heights <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math> respectively. The line joining their tops makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the ground. Then the distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>)</mo></math> between the poles, is
question: Two poles standing on a horizontal ground are of heights <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math> respectively. The line joining their tops makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the ground. Then the distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>)</mo></math> between the poles, is
solution:
Let, DC be the pole of height 5 m and AB be the pole of height 10 m.

Also, let DM⊥AB, then ACDM is a rectangle and hence, AM=DC=5 m and BM=AB-AM=10-5=5 m and the distance between the poles is AC=MD=K (let).


In ΔBDM
tan15°=BMMD=5K

⇒K=5tan15°

⇒K=53+13-1×3+13+1

⇒K=53+1+233-1

⇒K=52+3 m.

subject: Mathematics
q: From the base of a pole of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> meter, the angle of elevation of the top of a tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The pole subtends an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> at the top of the tower. Then the height of the tower is:
question: From the base of a pole of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> meter, the angle of elevation of the top of a tower is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>. The pole subtends an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> at the top of the tower. Then the height of the tower is:
solution:
Plotting the diagram we get,

In ∆ABD

tan60°=20+yx     ...i

In ∆AEC

tan30°=yx    ...ii

From equation (i) & (ii),(i) (ii)  we get, tan60°tan30°=20+yy⇒31/3=20+yy

⇒20+yy=3⇒y=10

So, height of tower =20+y=30 m
